sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “May He Send You Help from the Sanctuary”

heb.paragraph: "May the LORD answer you (yaʿănek̅ā) when you are in distress" (v.1). A pre-battle blessing. The congregation prays for the king before he goes to war: may God answer, protect, send help, remember sacrifices, grant desires. The psalm is communal intercession for royal success.

refs.note: Saul’s pre-battle sacrifice — the ritual context the psalm assumes.
"Pray in the Spirit on all occasions" — the NT’s spiritual warfare parallel.

notes.note: "May the LORD answer you in the day of distress. May the God of Jacob protect you. May he send you help from the sanctuary." Three "may" petitions for the king.
"May he give you the desire of your heart. May we shout for joy over your victory." The congregation’s prayer is for shared triumph: your victory is our celebration.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the five "may he" petitions create a liturgical rhythm — each building on the last. Answer → protect → send help → remember → grant. The arc is from hearing to fulfilment.
Calvin: the congregation prays for the king because his success is their survival. Calvin: praying for leaders is not optional but strategic — their battles are ours.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "help from the sanctuary" means divine aid from Zion, where the ark of the covenant resided. The temple is the source of military aid — God fights from his throne.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the psalm is a liturgy — the people praying for their king. The "you" is the king; the "we" is the nation. Leadership and community are bound together.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the pre-battle liturgy shows that Israel’s warfare was theological: victory came from God, not from military strength. The psalm is a declaration of dependence.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "lift up our banners in the name of our God" — the banners identify which army this is. The name of God is the flag under which Israel fights.

hist.context: A liturgy of pre-battle intercession. The congregation blesses the king: may the LORD answer you (v.1), protect you (v.1), send help from the sanctuary (v.2), remember your sacrifices (v.3), give you your heart’s desire (v.4). "May we shout for joy over your victory and lift up our banners in the name of our God" (v.5).

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “Some Trust in Chariots; We Trust in the Name of the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name (shēm) of the LORD our God" (v.7). The psalm’s most famous verse and its theological centre. Military technology vs divine name. Chariots fall; the name stands. The "name" is God’s character, reputation, and power — the sum of who he is.

refs.note: "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses" — the prophetic development of the same trust theology.
"The battle is not yours, but God’s" — the same principle in narrative form.

notes.note: "Now this I know: the LORD gives victory to his anointed." The shift from petition to declaration. Faith speaks in the present tense: I KNOW.
"Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God." The defining contrast. Technology vs theology. Hardware vs the name.
"They are brought to their knees and fall, but we rise up and stand firm." The result of trust: the militarily superior collapse; the theologically dependent stand.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the contrast is cinematic: chariots and horses vs a name. The visual (armies) against the invisible (a word). The invisible wins.

Calvin: the choice between chariots and God’s name is the choice every generation faces. Calvin: whatever functions as your ultimate security IS your god. The psalm asks: is it technology or the LORD?

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "trust" (zāk̅ar) literally means "invoke/remember." Some invoke chariots; we invoke the LORD’s name. The battle is won by what you call upon.
The NET: "brought to their knees and fall" vs "rise up and stand firm" — two opposite trajectories determined by one choice: where trust was placed.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: chariots were the supreme military technology — the tanks of the ancient world. Trusting the name over chariots is trusting the invisible over the overwhelming visible.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm’s second half shifts from prayer to proclamation. The congregation has moved from asking to declaring. Prayer produced confidence.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "the name" is everything God has revealed about himself. To trust the name is to trust the character behind it. The name is not a magic word but a personal reality.

hist.context: Confidence declared: "Now this I know: the LORD gives victory to his anointed" (v.6). The pivotal verse: some trust in chariots and horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD (v.7). The result: "they are brought to their knees and fall, but we rise up and stand firm" (v.8). The psalm closes with a final petition: "LORD, give victory to the king! Answer us when we call!" (v.9).

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in May the LORD Answer You.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNow this I know: The Lord gives victory to his anointed. He answers him from his heavenly sanctuary with the victorious power of his right hand.ESVNow I know that the LORD saves his anointed; he will answer him from his holy heaven with the saving might of his right hand.NIVThey are brought to their knees and fall, but we rise up and stand firm.ESVThey collapse and fall, but we rise and stand upright.

rec.text: ""May the LORD answer you in the day of distress. May the God of Jacob protect you. May he send you help from the sanctuary." Three "may" petitions for the king."

rows.label: The Blessing — 20:1–5
rows.text: "May the LORD answer you." Pre-battle intercession for the king. Community depends on its leader.

hebtext.note: "May the LORD answer you (yaʿănek̅ā) when you are in distress" (v.1). A pre-battle blessing. The congregation prays for the king before he goes to war: may God answer, protect, send help, remember sacrifices, grant desires. The psalm is communal intercession for royal success.

"Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name (shēm) of the LORD our God" (v.7). The psalm’s most famous verse and its theological centre. Military technology vs divine name. Chariots fall; the name stands. The "name" is God’s character, reputation, and power — the sum of who he is.

thread.text: Saul’s pre-battle sacrifice — the ritual context the psalm assumes.
"Pray in the Spirit on all occasions" — the NT’s spiritual warfare parallel.
"Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, who rely on horses" — the prophetic development of the same trust theology.
"The battle is not yours, but God’s" — the same principle in narrative form.

themes.note: Faith peaks: trust in the name over military technology. Sovereignty: the LORD gives victory to his anointed. The psalm defines where trust belongs.
